parts.heading: A Witch's Name
parts.body:
Imagine a ship, one of the fastest ever built, named after a fictional witch who, in a flash of speed, snatched a horse's tail! That's the surprising true origin of the Cutty Sark's name. She was christened after Nannie Dee, the witch from Robert Burns's 1791 poem, "Tam o' Shanter," who wore only a short shirt, or "cutty sark" in Broad Scots.

The association was simple and brilliant: the swiftness of the fictional witch mirrored the incredible speed of the clipper ship. In fact, the ship's figurehead, attributed to carver Fredrick Hellyer, is a stark white carving of a bare-breasted Nannie Dee herself, holding a grey horse's tail. Originally, other scantily clad witches from the poem followed along the bow, but these were later removed by the ship's owner, Jock Willis, in deference to 'good taste'.
parts.pullQuote: Imagine a ship, one of the fastest ever built, named after a fictional witch who, in a flash of speed, snatched a horse's tail!

parts.heading: Built for Speed
parts.body:
Cutty Sark was specifically ordered in 1869 by the Jock Willis Shipping Line for the lucrative tea trade. This was a highly competitive business where speed was paramount; faster ships commanded higher freight rates, and their names were even used in tea merchants' advertising.

The ship was designed to outdo the record-setting clipper Thermopylae. Willis chose Hercules Linton to design and build her, drawing inspiration from his own exceptionally fast ship, The Tweed, particularly its bow shape. However, Linton modified the stern, giving Cutty Sark a squarer design for better buoyancy in heavy seas.

The contract for construction, signed on February 1, 1869, with Scott & Linton, specified completion within six months at a highly competitive price of £17 per ton. The firm of Scott & Linton, however, ran out of money, leading to William Denny & Brothers taking over the contract. Cutty Sark was finally launched on November 22, 1869, a few months behind schedule.
parts.pullQuote: The ship was designed to outdo the record-setting clipper Thermopylae.

parts.heading: A Cutting-Edge Hull
parts.body:
Cutty Sark's construction was a marvel of 19th-century shipbuilding. Her hull featured an innovative composite design: a wooden hull built on a wrought-iron frame. This combination created a strong, rigid ship that resisted 'racking'—where the frame rectangles would become parallelograms—and resulted in less leakage.

Less leaking meant less crew time spent on pumping, freeing them up for sail changes, which was crucial for speed. The iron frame also occupied less cargo space than an all-wood hull. The bottom of the ship was crafted from American rock elm, while the visible parts above the waterline used East India teak. To further enhance her speed and reduce drag, the hull was covered with Muntz metal sheeting, which significantly reduced fouling.
parts.pullQuote: Her hull featured an innovative composite design: a wooden hull built on a wrought-iron frame.

parts.heading: The Race Against Steam
parts.body:
Cutty Sark's maiden voyage in February 1870 with Captain George Moodie, carrying wine and spirits to Shanghai, coincided with a momentous event: the opening of the Suez Canal in 1869. This canal dramatically shortened the route for steamships to China by about 3,300 nautical miles, making their voyages faster and more predictable.

While clippers still had to brave the longer route around the Cape of Good Hope, steamship technology had also made significant advancements, particularly in fuel efficiency. This meant steamships could now compete with, and often surpass, clippers in speed and reliability, commanding higher freight rates and lower insurance premiums for their cargoes. The tea trade quickly became dominated by steamers, forcing many clippers, including Cutty Sark, to adapt.
parts.pullQuote: Cutty Sark's maiden voyage in February 1870 with Captain George Moodie, carrying wine and spirits to Shanghai, coincided with a momentous event: the opening of the Suez Canal in 1869.

parts.heading: Rudder Lost, Honor Gained
parts.body:
The Cutty Sark's most famous challenge came in 1872 during a race against her rival, Thermopylae. Leaving Shanghai together on June 18, Cutty Sark initially built a lead of 400 nautical miles. However, disaster struck after passing through the Sunda Strait when a heavy gale ripped away her rudder.

Despite an order to put into Cape Town for repairs, Captain Moodie refused. Instead, the ship's carpenter, Henry Henderson, bravely constructed a new rudder from spare timbers and iron amidst gales and heavy seas. This incredible feat took six days, during which the crew and even the captain's son suffered injuries. Though she arrived in London a week after Thermopylae, completing the 122-day passage, the captain and crew were commended, and Henderson received a £50 bonus for his exceptional work. This marked Captain Moodie's last trip before transferring to steamships, a clear sign of the changing times.
parts.pullQuote: Despite an order to put into Cape Town for repairs, Captain Moodie refused.

parts.heading: The Wool Trade Years
parts.body:
By the late 1870s, the tea trade was no longer profitable for sailing ships, and Cutty Sark had to seek new cargoes. In December 1883, she embarked on a new career, departing Newcastle, New South Wales, with 4,289 bales of wool and 12 casks of tallow, arriving in London in an impressive 83 days. This was 25 days faster than her nearest rival that year.

This heralded the start of a decade-long period where Cutty Sark dominated the Australian wool trade. Under the command of Captain Richard Woodget from 1885 to 1893, she consistently broke records. Woodget achieved this by taking a more southerly route, harnessing the powerful 'Roaring Forties' winds, despite the ever-present danger of icebergs and severe gales. For ten years, Cutty Sark remained the fastest ship on the wool trade, a testament to her enduring speed even as steamships encroached on this route.
parts.pullQuote: For ten years, Cutty Sark remained the fastest ship on the wool trade, a testament to her enduring speed even as steamships encroached on this route.

parts.heading: A New Flag, A New Purpose
parts.body:
With steamships eventually dominating the wool trade, Cutty Sark's profitable sailing days were numbered. In 1895, Jock Willis sold her to the Portuguese firm Joaquim Antunes Ferreira for £1,250, and she was renamed Ferreira. Her crews affectionately called her 'Pequena Camisola,' a direct translation of 'cutty sark.'

She continued as a cargo ship, trading between Portugal, Rio de Janeiro, New Orleans, Mozambique, Angola, and Britain. In May 1916, a severe storm off the Cape of Good Hope dismasted her, necessitating a lengthy re-rigging to a barquentine sail arrangement over 18 months, due to the difficulty of obtaining materials during World War I. By 1922, Ferreira was the last clipper still operating anywhere in the world. Spotted in Falmouth harbour by retired sea captain Wilfred Dowman, her journey was far from over.
parts.pullQuote: By 1922, Ferreira was the last clipper still operating anywhere in the world.

parts.heading: A Training Legacy
parts.body:
Determined to save the historic vessel, Captain Wilfred Dowman purchased Cutty Sark for £3,750, with the support of his wife, Catharine Dowman. He meticulously restored her rigging to an approximation of its original glory and began using her as a cadet training ship from Falmouth, Cornwall.

After Dowman's death in 1936, Catharine Dowman generously gifted the ship, along with £5,000 for maintenance, to the Incorporated Thames Nautical Training College, HMS Worcester, at Greenhithe. Here, Cutty Sark served as an auxiliary vessel for sail training drills. By 1950, however, she was deemed surplus to requirements. In 1952, a tanker collision in the Thames caused damage to her bow, figurehead, and jibboom, though she was subsequently repaired.
parts.pullQuote: Determined to save the historic vessel, Captain Wilfred Dowman purchased Cutty Sark for £3,750, with the support of his wife, Catharine Dowman.

parts.heading: Permanent Dry Dock
parts.body:
In 1953, Cutty Sark was entrusted to the Cutty Sark Preservation Society. The following year, 1954, she was meticulously moved to a custom-built dry dock in Greenwich, London, for permanent public display. To prepare her for the journey and the dry dock, she was stripped of upper masts, yards, deck-houses, and ballast to lighten her. The skipper for this momentous tow was 83-year-old Captain C.E. Irving, who had sailed the world three times in her before he was 17.

Today, Cutty Sark stands as a proud museum ship, a central part of the National Historic Fleet and a popular tourist attraction. She is a prominent landmark in Greenwich, near the National Maritime Museum, and she even marks the finish of The Big Half marathon. Her signal flags, 'JKWS,' represent her code in the International Code of Signals, a constant reminder of her rich maritime heritage.
parts.pullQuote: Today, Cutty Sark stands as a proud museum ship, a central part of the National Historic Fleet and a popular tourist attraction.
